khan_exercises.title: Common prepositions
khan_exercises.description: Let's learn about the most common prepositions.
topic_path: Parts of speech: the preposition and the conjunction

items.question_content:
**Choose the preposition that makes the most sense in this sentence.**

>The passenger \_\_\_\_\_\_ the train was reading.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
In English, certain types of prepositions are used in certain situations.

For example:

* **To** and **from** describe the *direction* something moves.
* **By** and **on** describe the *position* of something.
* **Before** and **after** describe *when* something happened.
* **With** and **of** can link ideas and show possession.

What's the best preposition to use here?
Since we're describing the *position* of the passenger, we should use the preposition **"on"**.

What's the best preposition to use here?

khan_exercises.title: Compound prepositions
khan_exercises.description: Identify and use less common prepositions with multiple syllables.
topic_path: Parts of speech: the preposition and the conjunction

items.question_content:
**Choose the preposition that makes the most sense in this sentence.**

>The bunny hopped \_\_\_\_\_\_ the forest path.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Compound prepositions are longer and usually not as frequently used in Standard American English as common prepositions are. 

Some examples include _about, under, outside, inside, without, before, after, onto, into, beside, upon, beyond, against, among, below, until_.
We're looking for a preposition that describes the position and direction of the bunny, so we should use **"along"**.

khan_exercises.title: Prepositional phrases
khan_exercises.description: Identify prepositional phrases and how to use them!
topic_path: Parts of speech: the preposition and the conjunction

items.question_content:
**Identify the prepositional phrase.**

>The third little pig built a house of bricks.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Prepositional phrases begin with a preposition and can act as adjectives, nouns, or adverbs. Examples of prepositions include _on, off, to, of, in, out, after, before, under, over, by,_ and _with_.

>*Example:* The box was made **of heavy wood.**

In this example, the prepositional phrase "of heavy wood" acts as an adjective describing the "box".
**"Of bricks"** is the prepositional phrase in this sentence.

items.question_content:
**Identify the prepositional phrase.**

>I’d like a glass of orange juice, please.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Prepositional phrases begin with a preposition and can act as adjectives, nouns, or adverbs. Examples of prepositions include _on, off, to, of, in, out, after, before, under, over, by,_ and _with_.

>*Example:* The box was made **of heavy wood.**

In this example, the prepositional phrase "of heavy wood" acts as an adjective describing the "box".
**"Of orange juice"** is the prepositional phrase in this sentence.

items.question_content:
**Identify the prepositional phrase.**

>Ryo sang with glee during his solo at the recital.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Prepositional phrases begin with a preposition and can act as adjectives, nouns, or adverbs. Examples of prepositions include _on, off, to, of, in, out, after, before, under, over, by,_ and _with_.

>*Example:* The box was made **of heavy wood.**

In this example, the prepositional phrase "of heavy wood" acts as an adjective describing the "box".
**"With glee"** is the prepositional phrase in this sentence.

items.question_content:

hints:

>*Example:* The box was made **of heavy wood.**

In this example, the prepositional phrase "of heavy wood" acts as an adjective describing the "box".
**"With ease"** is the prepositional phrase in this sentence.
